real_questions: Looking at liquid assets as a percentage of total assets, if a large portion is in held-for-sale loans, how does that factor into liquidity needs?
Through the 2023 crisis, how did deposits hold up?
Was there any flight to quality?
What are your plans for debt issuance going forward?
Given the market demand for whole loan sales, what has pricing been looking like in the last 12 months?
Typically when banks try to build direct deposit franchises, they end up with high-net-worth customers attracted to elevated rates who leave once rates come down — is that the case with your bank?
What contingent liquidity access do you have?
common_pitfalls: Issuers overstate held-for-sale loans as a liquidity source. S&P treats them as not truly liquid in stress.
Rate-chasing direct deposit franchises consistently get overstated as durable. S&P flags these as flight-prone.
strong_answer_markers: Strong answers on the 2023 stress name specific flight-to-quality dynamics in the deposit base, not just aggregate balance numbers.
agency_intel: S&P presses the link between whole-loan secondary market liquidity and balance-sheet liquidity. If the secondary market shuts, the held-for-sale book becomes an asset, not a liquidity source.
